member.name: T:DrawnUi.Camera.AudioSampleConverter
M:DrawnUi.Camera.AudioSampleConverter.#ctor(System.Int32,System.Single,System.Int32)
member.summary: Creates a new audio preprocessor.
member.param: Desired output sample rate in Hz (e.g. 24000 for OpenAI, 16000 for Whisper).
RMS level (0..1) below which audio is considered silence. Set to 0 to disable silence gating.
Number of consecutive silent chunks before audio is suppressed. At 480 samples/48kHz, 100 chunks is roughly 1 second.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.AudioSampleConverter.SetFormat(System.Int32,System.Int32)
member.summary: Set the source audio format. Call before Process(), and again if the audio device changes.
member.param: Source sample rate in Hz (e.g. 44100, 48000).
Number of channels (1=mono, 2=stereo).

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.AudioSampleConverter.Reset
member.summary: Resets resampling and silence gate state. Call when starting a new recording/streaming session.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.AudioSampleConverter.Process(System.Byte[])
member.summary: Process a chunk of raw PCM16 audio. Applies downmix, silence gate, and resampling as needed. Each step is skipped when input already matches the target (zero-copy passthrough).
member.param: Raw PCM16 audio bytes (little-endian, interleaved channels).
member.returns: Processed mono PCM16 audio at target sample rate, or null if the chunk should be skipped (silence or invalid input).

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.AudioSampleConverter.CalculateRms(System.Byte[])
member.summary: Fast RMS calculation on PCM16 mono data. Returns normalized 0..1 value.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.AudioSampleConverter.DownmixToMono(System.Byte[],System.Int32)
member.summary: Downmixes multi-channel PCM16 to mono by averaging all channels per frame.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.AudioSampleConverter.Resample(System.Byte[],System.Int32,System.Int32)
member.summary: Resamples PCM16 mono audio using linear interpolation. Maintains continuity across calls via _resamplePosition.

member.name: T:DrawnUi.Camera.Mp4MetadataInjector
member.summary:
Injects metadata (GPS location, author, camera model, date, etc.) into MP4/MOV video files. Writes both QuickTime udta text atoms (moov > udta) and Apple mdta metadata (moov > meta > hdlr+keys+ilst). The Apple mdta format is required for iOS Photos to display camera info. Uses stream-based I/O — only the moov box (typically a few KB) is read into memory.
Supported atoms: ©xyz (GPS), ©ART (artist/author), ©nam (title), ©too (software), ©cmt (comment), ©day (date), ©des (description), ©mak (make), ©mod (model), and any other ©-prefixed text atom.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.Mp4MetadataInjector.InjectMetadata(System.String,DrawnUi.Camera.Metadata)
member.summary: Injects metadata from a Metadata object into an MP4/MOV file. Maps Metadata properties to MP4 udta text atoms. Modifies the file in-place. Only reads the moov box into memory (typically a few KB).

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.Mp4MetadataInjector.InjectMetadataAsync(System.String,DrawnUi.Camera.Metadata)
member.summary: Injects metadata from a Metadata object into an MP4/MOV file. Maps Metadata properties to MP4 udta text atoms. Modifies the file in-place. Only reads the moov box into memory (typically a few KB).

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.Mp4MetadataInjector.InjectAtoms(System.String,System.Collections.Generic.IReadOnlyDictionary{System.String,System.String})
member.summary: Injects arbitrary text atoms into an MP4/MOV file's moov > udta box. Keys are FourCC strings (e.g., "©ART"), values are UTF-8 text. Existing atoms of the same type are replaced; new ones are appended.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.Mp4MetadataInjector.InjectAtomsAsync(System.String,System.Collections.Generic.IReadOnlyDictionary{System.String,System.String})
member.summary: Injects arbitrary text atoms into an MP4/MOV file's moov > udta box. Also injects Apple mdta metadata for iOS Photos camera info display. Keys are FourCC strings (e.g., "©ART"), values are UTF-8 text. Existing atoms of the same type are replaced; new ones are appended.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.Mp4MetadataInjector.InjectLocation(System.String,System.Double,System.Double)
member.summary: Injects GPS location into an MP4/MOV file (convenience wrapper).

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.Mp4MetadataInjector.InjectLocationAsync(System.String,System.Double,System.Double)
member.summary: Injects GPS location into an MP4/MOV file (convenience wrapper).

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.Mp4MetadataInjector.ReadLocation(System.String,System.Double@,System.Double@)
member.summary: Reads GPS location from an MP4/MOV file if present.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.Mp4MetadataInjector.ReadAtoms(System.String)
member.summary: Reads all recognized text atoms from the moov > udta box. Keys are FourCC strings (e.g., "©ART"), values are UTF-8 text. Returns null if the file has no moov or udta box.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.Mp4MetadataInjector.MetadataToAtoms(DrawnUi.Camera.Metadata)
member.summary: Converts a Metadata object into a dictionary of MP4 udta text atoms.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.Mp4MetadataInjector.AtomsToMetadata(System.Collections.Generic.IReadOnlyDictionary{System.String,System.String},DrawnUi.Camera.Metadata)
member.summary: Populates a Metadata object from MP4 udta text atoms read from a file.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.Mp4MetadataInjector.InjectAtomsIntoMoov(System.Byte[],System.Collections.Generic.List{System.ValueTuple{System.UInt32,System.Byte[]}})
member.summary: Injects multiple atoms into the moov byte array. Processes sequentially — each atom insertion updates the byte array before the next.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.Mp4MetadataInjector.InjectSingleAtom(System.Byte[],System.UInt32,System.Byte[])
member.summary: Injects a single atom into moov bytes. Finds or creates udta, then replaces existing atom of the same type or appends a new one.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.Mp4MetadataInjector.BuildAppleMetaBox(System.Collections.Generic.IReadOnlyDictionary{System.String,System.String})
member.summary: Builds an Apple-format meta box (moov > meta) with hdlr(mdta) + keys + ilst structure. iOS Photos reads this format for camera make, model, software, creation date display. Returns null if no atoms have Apple key mappings.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.Mp4MetadataInjector.InjectAppleMetaBoxIntoMoov(System.Byte[],System.Byte[])
member.summary: Injects or replaces the Apple-format meta box in moov bytes. The meta box is a direct child of moov (not inside udta).

member.name: T:DrawnUi.Camera.PrerecordingEncodedBuffer
member.summary:
Two-buffer pre-recording system for H.264 encoded video frames.
Maintains two fixed-size byte[] buffers (~13.5 MB each) with automatic rotation. When one buffer exceeds PreRecordDuration, swaps to the other buffer atomically. Enables zero-drop pre-recording with minimal locking (~100ns per frame append).
Memory efficiency: - H.264 @ 1080p 30fps: ~75 KB/frame ? 150 frames/5s = ~11.25 MB - Buffer size: 11.25 MB � 1.2 (IDR headroom) = ~13.5 MB per buffer - Total: ~27 MB vs 1.245 GB uncompressed (46:1 compression!)

member.summary: Stores a single encoded frame with timing information

member.summary: Tracks state of each buffer independently

member.param: Maximum duration to maintain in buffer (e.g., 5 seconds)

member.summary: Gets current buffered duration (difference between newest and oldest frame timestamps) IMPORTANT: Uses actual frame PTS timestamps, not wall-clock time!

member.name: T:DrawnUi.Camera.PrerecordingEncodedBufferApple
member.summary:
Two-buffer pre-recording system for H.264 encoded video frames.
Maintains two byte buffers sized dynamically from target bitrate and retention window. When data reaches the configured time horizon the buffers swap atomically, ensuring pre-roll retention without dropping keyframes.

member.name: T:DrawnUi.Camera.AndroidCaptureVideoEncoder
member.summary:
Android implementation of capture video encoding using MediaCodec with Surface input (GPU path).
Two Modes (mirrors iOS AppleVideoToolboxEncoder): 1. Normal Recording: MediaCodec → MediaMuxer → MP4 2. Pre-Recording: MediaCodec → PrerecordingEncodedBuffer (circular buffer in memory) On record press: Write buffer + live frames to ONE MediaMuxer session
Pipeline (Normal Recording): Skia → EGLSurface → MediaCodec → MediaMuxer → MP4
Pipeline (Pre-Recording): Skia → EGLSurface → MediaCodec → PrerecordingEncodedBuffer (memory) User presses record → MediaMuxer: write buffer (PTS 0-3s) + live (PTS 3s-7s) → ONE MP4

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.AndroidCaptureVideoEncoder.SetAudioCodec(System.String)
member.summary: Sets the preferred audio codec name to use. Android primarily supports AAC, but this allows future codec expansion.

member.name: P:DrawnUi.Camera.AndroidCaptureVideoEncoder.SharedPreRecordingBuffer
member.summary: Optional pre-allocated buffer provided by SkiaCamera. When set, encoder will reuse this buffer instead of allocating a new one, eliminating the ~27MB allocation lag spike when pre-recording starts.

member.summary: Event fired when a GPU frame has been successfully processed and encoded. Used by SkiaCamera to track recording FPS.

member.summary: Request immediate keyframe from encoder (Bundle.PARAMETER_KEY_REQUEST_SYNC_FRAME) Used when transitioning from pre-recording to live recording

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.AndroidCaptureVideoEncoder.WaitForKeyFrameAsync(System.TimeSpan)
member.summary: Wait for next keyframe to arrive in buffer (with timeout) Returns true if keyframe arrived, false if timeout

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.AndroidCaptureVideoEncoder.WarmUpEncoderAsync
member.summary:
Professional encoder warm-up pattern (how Instagram/Snapchat/TikTok avoid frame loss).
Submits a dummy black frame to trigger MediaCodec initialization, then waits for FORMAT_CHANGED event. This ensures encoder is fully ready before any real frames arrive.
Called during InitializeAsync (when camera preview starts), NOT when recording starts. User sees camera preview while encoder warms up in background (transparent to user).
GUARANTEE: Zero frame loss when recording starts.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.CubicSpline.GetNextXIndex(System.Single)
member.summary: Find where in xOrig the specified x falls, by simultaneous traverse. This allows xs to be less than x[0] and/or greater than x[n-1]. So allows extrapolation. This keeps state, so requires that x be sorted and xs called in ascending order, and is not multi-thread safe.

member.summary: Fit x,y and then eval at points xs and return the corresponding y's. This does the "natural spline" style for ends. This can extrapolate off the ends of the splines. You must provide points in X sort order.
member.param: Input. X coordinates to fit.
Input. Y coordinates to fit.
Input. X coordinates to evaluate the fitted curve at.
Optional slope constraint for the first point. Single.NaN means no constraint.
Optional slope constraint for the final point. Single.NaN means no constraint.
Turn on console output. Default is false.

member.summary: Compute spline coefficients for the specified x,y points. This does the "natural spline" style for ends. This can extrapolate off the ends of the splines. You must provide points in X sort order.
member.param: Input. X coordinates to fit.
Input. Y coordinates to fit.
Optional slope constraint for the first point. Single.NaN means no constraint.
Optional slope constraint for the final point. Single.NaN means no constraint.
Turn on console output. Default is false.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.CubicSpline.Eval(System.Single[],System.Boolean)
member.summary: Evaluate the spline at the specified x coordinates. This can extrapolate off the ends of the splines. You must provide X's in ascending order. The spline must already be computed before calling this, meaning you must have already called Fit() or FitAndEval().
member.param: Input. X coordinates to evaluate the fitted curve at.
Turn on console output. Default is false.
member.returns: The computed y values for each x.

member.summary: Evaluate (compute) the slope of the spline at the specified x coordinates. This can extrapolate off the ends of the splines. You must provide X's in ascending order. The spline must already be computed before calling this, meaning you must have already called Fit() or FitAndEval().
member.param: Input. X coordinates to evaluate the fitted curve at.
Turn on console output. Default is false.
member.returns: The computed y values for each x.

member.summary: Static all-in-one method to fit the splines and evaluate at X coordinates.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.CubicSpline.FitParametric(System.Single[],System.Single[],System.Int32,System.Single[]@,System.Single[]@,System.Single,System.Single,System.Single,System.Single)
member.summary: Fit the input x,y points using the parametric approach, so that y does not have to be an explicit function of x, meaning there does not need to be a single value of y for each x.
member.param: Input x coordinates.
Input y coordinates.
How many output points to create.
Output (interpolated) x values.
Output (interpolated) y values.

Optionally specifies the first point's slope in combination with firstDy. Together they are a vector describing the direction of the parametric spline of the starting point. The vector does not need to be normalized. If either is NaN then neither is used.
See description of dx0.

Optionally specifies the last point's slope in combination with lastDy. Together they are a vector describing the direction of the parametric spline of the last point. The vector does not need to be normalized. If either is NaN then neither is used.
See description of dxN.

member.name: T:DrawnUi.Camera.GlPreviewRenderer
member.summary: GPU-based preview renderer that replaces the RenderScript/ImageReader preview pipeline. Uses a dedicated GL thread with EGL pbuffer surface, SurfaceTexture for zero-copy camera frames, and OES shader for GPU-native YUV→RGB conversion. Mirrors AndroidCaptureVideoEncoder's GPU thread pattern but without encoding.

member.name: P:DrawnUi.Camera.GlPreviewRenderer.UseBlitPreview
member.summary: Dev flag: Use glBlitFramebuffer path (recording-style) instead of direct render + CPU Y-flip. Renders OES at camera resolution to a source FBO, then blits downscaled + Y-flipped to the readback FBO via glBlitFramebuffer. Eliminates CPU Y-flip and uses GPU bilinear filtering. Toggle to compare preview FPS between approaches.

member.name: E:DrawnUi.Camera.GlPreviewRenderer.PreviewFrameReady
member.summary: Fired when a new preview frame is ready. Parameters: SKImage (caller takes ownership), timestampNs from SurfaceTexture.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.GlPreviewRenderer.Initialize(System.Int32,System.Int32,System.Int32,System.Int32,System.Boolean,System.Int32)
member.summary: Initialize the GPU preview renderer. Creates EGL context, SurfaceTexture, and readback FBO. Must be called before Start(). The initialization runs on a temporary GL thread.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.GlPreviewRenderer.GetCameraOutputSurface
member.summary: Get the Surface for use as Camera2 output target.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.GlPreviewRenderer.Start
member.summary: Start the GL preview thread. Begins processing and delivering camera frames.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.GlPreviewRenderer.Stop
member.summary: Stop the GL preview thread. Resources are kept for restart.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.GlPreviewRenderer.OnCameraFrameAvailable(System.Object,Android.Graphics.SurfaceTexture)
member.summary: Called on arbitrary camera thread when a new frame is available from SurfaceTexture. Just signals the GL thread — no heavy work here.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.GlPreviewRenderer.GlPreviewLoop
member.summary: GL preview loop — runs on dedicated background thread. Same pattern as AndroidCaptureVideoEncoder.GpuEncodingLoop.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.GlPreviewRenderer.ProcessPreviewFrame
member.summary: Process a single preview frame on the GL thread. MakeCurrent → UpdateTexImage → OES render → readback → SKImage. Dispatches to Direct or Blit path based on UseBlitPreview flag.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.GlPreviewRenderer.ProcessPreviewFrameDirect
member.summary: DIRECT path (original): render OES at preview resolution → glReadPixels → CPU Y-flip → SKImage.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.GlPreviewRenderer.ProcessPreviewFrameBlit
member.summary: BLIT path (recording-style): render OES at camera resolution to source FBO → glBlitFramebuffer downscale + GPU Y-flip to readback FBO → glReadPixels small buffer. Mirrors AndroidCaptureVideoEncoder + GlPreviewScaler pipeline but without the encoder. Eliminates CPU Y-flip; GPU bilinear filtering handles downscale.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.GlPreviewRenderer.SetupEgl
member.summary: Create EGL display, context, and a 1x1 pbuffer surface. Pattern from AndroidCaptureVideoEncoder.SetupEglForCodecSurface but using EGL_PBUFFER_BIT.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.GlPreviewRenderer.SetupReadbackFbo(System.Int32,System.Int32)
member.summary: Create FBO + renderbuffer at preview resolution for readback. Same pattern as GlPreviewScaler.Initialize().

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.GlPreviewRenderer.SetupSourceFbo(System.Int32,System.Int32)
member.summary: Create FBO + renderbuffer at camera resolution for OES rendering (blit path source). The OES texture is rendered here at full camera resolution, then glBlitFramebuffer downscales + Y-flips to the readback FBO.

member.name: T:DrawnUi.Camera.GlPreviewScaler
member.summary:
GPU-native preview downscaler using GLES 3.0 glBlitFramebuffer. Mirrors Apple's MetalPreviewScaler pattern for Android.
Reads from the encoder's default FBO (0), blits with bilinear filtering to a smaller FBO, then reads back only the small buffer via glReadPixels. This avoids the expensive full-resolution GPU→CPU readback that Skia's Snapshot()+DrawImage approach triggers.
Performance: ~3-7ms per preview frame (vs 15-40ms with naive Skia readback).

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.GlPreviewScaler.Initialize(System.Int32,System.Int32,System.Int32,System.Int32)
member.summary: Initialize GL resources. Must be called on GL thread with active EGL context.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.GlPreviewScaler.ScaleAndReadback(System.Int32)
member.summary: Downscale the current encoder framebuffer (FBO 0) to preview size and read back as SKImage. Must be called on GL thread after canvas.Flush()+grContext.Flush(), before EglSwapBuffers. Returns null on failure.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.NativeCamera.StopGpuCameraSession
member.summary: Stop using GPU camera path and immediately create a new preview Camera2 session. Camera2 must always have a valid session with a valid surface — leaving it with a broken session (dead encoder surface) causes the device to error out. The GL preview thread is NOT started here — call ResumeGlPreview() after encoder is disposed.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.NativeCamera.ResumeGlPreview
member.summary: Start the GL preview thread to begin processing frames. Must be called AFTER encoder is fully disposed (its TearDownEgl affects EGL state). The Camera2 session is already targeting the GL preview surface (from StopGpuCameraSession).

member.name: P:DrawnUi.Camera.SkiaCamera.AudioCodecIndex
member.summary: Index of the audio codec to use. -1 for default (usually AAC). Use GetAvailableAudioCodecsAsync() to get the list of available codecs.

member.name: P:DrawnUi.Camera.SkiaCamera.EnableAudioRecording
member.summary: Whether to record audio with video. Default is true. Must be set before starting video recording.

member.name: P:DrawnUi.Camera.SkiaCamera.EnableVideoRecording
member.summary: Whether to record video frames. Default is true. When false, only audio will be recorded (output: M4A file). EnableAudioRecording must be true when EnableVideoRecording is false.

member.name: P:DrawnUi.Camera.SkiaCamera.EnableVideoPreview
member.summary: Whether to display video preview UI. Default is true. When false, hides the preview display but camera still initializes if EnableVideoRecording=true. Set both EnableVideoPreview=false AND EnableVideoRecording=false for pure audio-only mode (no camera hardware).

member.name: P:DrawnUi.Camera.SkiaCamera.EnableAudioMonitoring
member.summary: Whether to enable live audio preview/monitoring. Default is false. When true, provides live audio feedback (useful for audio level meters, live monitoring). Independent from EnableAudioRecording - you can monitor audio without recording it.

member.name: P:DrawnUi.Camera.SkiaCamera.UseRealtimeVideoProcessing
member.summary: Whether to use capture video flow (frame-by-frame processing) instead of native video recording. When true, individual camera frames are captured and processed through FrameProcessor callback before encoding. Default is false (use native video recording).

member.name: P:DrawnUi.Camera.SkiaCamera.EnablePreRecording
member.summary: Whether to buffer pre-recorded frames before video recording starts. When enabled, frames are buffered in memory before recording begins.

member.name: P:DrawnUi.Camera.SkiaCamera.PreRecordDuration
member.summary: Duration of pre-recording buffer to maintain (default: 3 seconds). Assumes 30 fps average frame rate for buffer size calculation.

member.name: F:DrawnUi.Camera.SkiaCamera.PreviewVideoFlowProperty
member.summary: Whether to mirror composed recording frames to the on-screen preview during capture video flow. Set this to true to see overlays exactly as recorded; set to false to avoid any mirroring overhead.

While recording, show exactly the frames being composed for the encoder as the on-screen preview. This avoids stutter by not relying on a separate preview feed. Controlled by MirrorRecordingToPreview.

member.name: P:DrawnUi.Camera.SkiaCamera.PreviewVideoFlow
member.summary: Controls whether preview shows processed frames from the video flow encoder (TRUE) or raw camera frames (FALSE). Only applies when UseRealtimeVideoProcessing is TRUE. Default is TRUE to show processed preview with overlays. Set to FALSE to show raw camera preview while still recording processed video with overlays.

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.SkiaCamera.ScheduleRestartDebounced
member.summary:
Debounces rapid restart requests using a settling period. Waits for property changes to settle before restarting the camera. This prevents camera restart spam when multiple properties change in quick succession.
How it works: - First property change: starts 500ms settling timer - More property changes during settling: timer resets to 500ms (waits for "peace") - No changes for 500ms: camera restarts once with all accumulated changes

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.SkiaCamera.GetRotationCorrectedDimensions(System.Int32,System.Int32)
member.summary: Gets rotation-corrected dimensions for encoder/recording based on platform and sensor orientation. Use this to align encoder dimensions with preview orientation.
member.param: Raw format width
Raw format height
member.returns: Tuple of (correctedWidth, correctedHeight)

Spot (10x10 points) or CenterWeighted (50x50 points)

member.name: M:DrawnUi.Camera.SkiaCamera.MeasureSceneBrightness(DrawnUi.Camera.MeteringMode,SkiaSharp.SKImage)
member.summary: Measures scene brightness using adaptive exposure bracketing to handle extreme lighting conditions. For bright outdoor conditions, uses progressively faster shutter speeds and lower ISO until non-clipped data is obtained. Falls back to histogram analysis of darkest pixels when complete saturation occurs.
member.param: Spot (10x10 points) or CenterWeighted (50x50 points)
member.returns: Brightness measurement result

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.System.Com.BLOB
member.summary: The BLOB structure (nspapi.h), which is derived from Binary Large Object, contains information about a block of data.

remarks.para:
The structure name BLOB comes from the acronym BLOB, which stands for Binary Large Object. This structure does not describe the nature of the data pointed to by pBlobData.
Note Windows Sockets defines a similar BLOB structure in Wtypes.h. Using both header files in the same source code file creates redefinition–compile time errors.
Read more on learn.microsoft.com.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.BLOB.cbSize
member.summary: Size of the block of data pointed to by pBlobData, in bytes.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.BLOB.pBlobData
member.summary: Pointer to a block of data.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.System.Com.CLSCTX
member.summary: Values that are used in activation calls to indicate the execution contexts in which an object is to be run.

remarks.para: Values from the CLSCTX enumeration are used in activation calls (CoCreateInstance, CoCreateInstanceEx, CoGetClassObject, and so on) to indicate the preferred execution contexts (in-process, local, or remote) in which an object is to be run. They are also used in calls to CoRegisterClassObject to indicate the set of execution contexts in which a class object is to be made available for requests to construct instances (IClassFactory::CreateInstance). To indicate that more than one context is acceptable, you can combine multiple values with Boolean ORs. The contexts are tried in the order in which they are listed.
Given a set of CLSCTX flags, the execution context to be used depends on the availability of registered class codes and other parameters according to the following algorithm.
This doc was truncated.
Read more on learn.microsoft.com.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.CLSCTX.CLSCTX_INPROC_SERVER
member.summary: The code that creates and manages objects of this class is a DLL that runs in the same process as the caller of the function specifying the class context.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.CLSCTX.CLSCTX_INPROC_HANDLER
member.summary: The code that manages objects of this class is an in-process handler. This is a DLL that runs in the client process and implements client-side structures of this class when instances of the class are accessed remotely.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.CLSCTX.CLSCTX_LOCAL_SERVER
member.summary: The EXE code that creates and manages objects of this class runs on same machine but is loaded in a separate process space.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.CLSCTX.CLSCTX_INPROC_SERVER16
member.summary: Obsolete.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.CLSCTX.CLSCTX_REMOTE_SERVER
member.summary: A remote context. The LocalServer32 or LocalService code that creates and manages objects of this class is run on a different computer.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.CLSCTX.CLSCTX_INPROC_HANDLER16
member.summary: Obsolete.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.CLSCTX.CLSCTX_NO_CODE_DOWNLOAD
member.summary: Disables the downloading of code from the directory service or the Internet. This flag cannot be set at the same time as CLSCTX_ENABLE_CODE_DOWNLOAD.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.CLSCTX.CLSCTX_RESERVED5
member.summary: Reserved.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.CLSCTX.CLSCTX_NO_CUSTOM_MARSHAL
member.summary: Specify if you want the activation to fail if it uses custom marshalling.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.CLSCTX.CLSCTX_ENABLE_CODE_DOWNLOAD
member.summary: Enables the downloading of code from the directory service or the Internet. This flag cannot be set at the same time as CLSCTX_NO_CODE_DOWNLOAD.

summary.para: The CLSCTX_NO_FAILURE_LOG can be used to override the logging of failures in CoCreateInstanceEx. If the ActivationFailureLoggingLevel is created, the following values can determine the status of event logging:

Disables activate-as-activator (AAA) activations for this activation only. This flag overrides the setting of the EOAC_DISABLE_AAA flag from the EOLE_AUTHENTICATION_CAPABILITIES enumeration. This flag cannot be set at the same time as CLSCTX_ENABLE_AAA. Any activation where a server process would be launched under the caller's identity is known as an activate-as-activator (AAA) activation. Disabling AAA activations allows an application that runs under a privileged account (such as LocalSystem) to help prevent its identity from being used to launch untrusted components. Library applications that use activation calls should always set this flag during those calls. This helps prevent the library application from being used in an escalation-of-privilege security attack. This is the only way to disable AAA activations in a library application because the EOAC_DISABLE_AAA flag from the EOLE_AUTHENTICATION_CAPABILITIES enumeration is applied only to the server process and not to the library application. Windows 2000: This flag is not supported.

Enables activate-as-activator (AAA) activations for this activation only. This flag overrides the setting of the EOAC_DISABLE_AAA flag from the EOLE_AUTHENTICATION_CAPABILITIES enumeration. This flag cannot be set at the same time as CLSCTX_DISABLE_AAA. Any activation where a server process would be launched under the caller's identity is known as an activate-as-activator (AAA) activation. Enabling this flag allows an application to transfer its identity to an activated component. Windows 2000: This flag is not supported.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.CLSCTX.CLSCTX_FROM_DEFAULT_CONTEXT
member.summary: Begin this activation from the default context of the current apartment.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.CLSCTX.CLSCTX_ACTIVATE_32_BIT_SERVER
member.summary: Activate or connect to a 32-bit version of the server; fail if one is not registered.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.CLSCTX.CLSCTX_ACTIVATE_64_BIT_SERVER
member.summary: Activate or connect to a 64 bit version of the server; fail if one is not registered.

summary.para: When this flag is specified, COM uses the impersonation token of the thread, if one is present, for the activation request made by the thread. When this flag is not specified or if the thread does not have an impersonation token, COM uses the process token of the thread's process for the activation request made by the thread.
Windows Vista or later: This flag is supported.
Indicates activation is for an app container.

para.div: Note This flag is reserved for internal use and is not intended to be used directly from your code.

summary.para: Specify this flag for Interactive User activation behavior for As-Activator servers. A strongly named Medium IL Windows Store app can use this flag to launch an "As Activator" COM server without a strong name. Also, you can use this flag to bind to a running instance of the COM server that's launched by a desktop application. The client must be Medium IL, it must be strongly named, which means that it has a SysAppID in the client token, it can't be in session 0, and it must have the same user as the session ID's user in the client token. If the server is out-of-process and "As Activator", it launches the server with the token of the client token's session user. This token won't be strongly named. If the server is out-of-process and RunAs "Interactive User", this flag has no effect. If the server is out-of-process and is any other RunAs type, the activation fails. This flag has no effect for in-process servers. Off-machine activations fail when they use this flag.

para.div: Note This flag is reserved for internal use and is not intended to be used directly from your code.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.System.Com.FUNCFLAGS
member.summary: Specifies function flags.

remarks.para: FUNCFLAG_FHIDDEN means that the property should never be shown in object browsers, property browsers, and so on. This function is useful for removing items from an object model. Code can bind to the member, but the user will never know that the member exists. FUNCFLAG_FNONBROWSABLE means that the property should not be displayed in a properties browser. It is used in circumstances in which an error would occur if the property were shown in a properties browser. FUNCFLAG_FRESRICTED means that macro-oriented programmers should not be allowed to access this member. These members are usually treated as _FHIDDEN by tools such as Visual Basic, with the main difference being that code cannot bind to those members.
Read more on learn.microsoft.com.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.FUNCFLAGS.FUNCFLAG_FRESTRICTED
member.summary: The function should not be accessible from macro languages. This flag is intended for system-level functions or functions that type browsers should not display.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.FUNCFLAGS.FUNCFLAG_FSOURCE
member.summary: The function returns an object that is a source of events.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.FUNCFLAGS.FUNCFLAG_FBINDABLE
member.summary: The function that supports data binding.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.FUNCFLAGS.FUNCFLAG_FREQUESTEDIT
member.summary: When set, any call to a method that sets the property results first in a call to IPropertyNotifySink::OnRequestEdit. The implementation of OnRequestEdit determines if the call is allowed to set the property.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.FUNCFLAGS.FUNCFLAG_FDISPLAYBIND
member.summary: The function that is displayed to the user as bindable. FUNC_FBINDABLE must also be set.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.FUNCFLAGS.FUNCFLAG_FDEFAULTBIND
member.summary: The function that best represents the object. Only one function in a type information can have this attribute.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.FUNCFLAGS.FUNCFLAG_FHIDDEN
member.summary: The function should not be displayed to the user, although it exists and is bindable.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.FUNCFLAGS.FUNCFLAG_FUSESGETLASTERROR
member.summary: The function supports GetLastError. If an error occurs during the function, the caller can call GetLastError to retrieve the error code.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.FUNCFLAGS.FUNCFLAG_FDEFAULTCOLLELEM
member.summary: Permits an optimization in which the compiler looks for a member named xyz on the type of abc. If such a member is found and is flagged as an accessor function for an element of the default collection, then a call is generated to that member function. Permitted on members in dispinterfaces and interfaces; not permitted on modules. For more information, refer to defaultcollelem in Type Libraries and the Object Description Language.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.FUNCFLAGS.FUNCFLAG_FUIDEFAULT
member.summary: The type information member is the default member for display in the user interface.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.FUNCFLAGS.FUNCFLAG_FNONBROWSABLE
member.summary: The property appears in an object browser, but not in a properties browser.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.FUNCFLAGS.FUNCFLAG_FREPLACEABLE
member.summary: Tags the interface as having default behaviors.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.FUNCFLAGS.FUNCFLAG_FIMMEDIATEBIND
member.summary: Mapped as individual bindable properties.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.System.Com.INVOKEKIND
member.summary: Specifies the way a function is invoked.
member.remarks: In C, value assignment is written as *pobj1 = *pobj2, while reference assignment is written as pobj1 = pobj2. Other languages have other syntactic conventions. A property or data member can support only a value assignment, a reference assignment, or both. The INVOKEKIND enumeration constants are the same constants that are passed to IDispatch::Invoke to specify the way in which a function is invoked.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.INVOKEKIND.INVOKE_FUNC
member.summary: The member is called using a normal function invocation syntax.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.INVOKEKIND.INVOKE_PROPERTYGET
member.summary: The function is invoked using a normal property-access syntax.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.INVOKEKIND.INVOKE_PROPERTYPUT
member.summary: The function is invoked using a property value assignment syntax. Syntactically, a typical programming language might represent changing a property in the same way as assignment. For example: object.property : = value.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.INVOKEKIND.INVOKE_PROPERTYPUTREF
member.summary: The function is invoked using a property reference assignment syntax.

member.name: M:Windows.Win32.System.Com.IUnknown.AddRef
member.summary: Increments the reference count for an interface pointer to a COM object. You should call this method whenever you make a copy of an interface pointer.
member.returns: The method returns the new reference count. This value is intended to be used only for test purposes.

remarks.para: A COM object uses a per-interface reference-counting mechanism to ensure that the object doesn't outlive references to it. You use **AddRef** to stabilize a copy of an interface pointer. It can also be called when the life of a cloned pointer must extend beyond the lifetime of the original pointer. The cloned pointer must be released by calling [IUnknown::Release](/windows/desktop/api/unknwn/nf-unknwn-iunknown-queryinterface(refiid_void)) on it. The internal reference counter that **AddRef** maintains should be a 32-bit unsigned integer.
Read more on learn.microsoft.com.

member.name: M:Windows.Win32.System.Com.IUnknown.Release
member.summary: Decrements the reference count for an interface on a COM object.
member.returns: The method returns the new reference count. This value is intended to be used only for test purposes.

remarks.para: When the reference count on an object reaches zero, **Release** must cause the interface pointer to free itself. When the released pointer is the only (formerly) outstanding reference to an object (whether the object supports single or multiple interfaces), the implementation must free the object. Note that aggregation of objects restricts the ability to recover interface pointers.
Read more on learn.microsoft.com.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.IUnknown.IID_Guid
member.summary: The IID guid for this interface.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.System.Com.LOCKTYPE
member.summary: The LOCKTYPE enumeration values indicate the type of locking requested for the specified range of bytes. The values are used in the ILockBytes::LockRegion and IStream::LockRegion methods.

remarks.para: Learn more about this API from learn.microsoft.com.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.LOCKTYPE.LOCK_WRITE
member.summary: If this lock is granted, the specified range of bytes can be opened and read any number of times, but writing to the locked range is prohibited except for the owner that was granted this lock.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.LOCKTYPE.LOCK_EXCLUSIVE
member.summary: If this lock is granted, writing to the specified range of bytes is prohibited except by the owner that was granted this lock.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.LOCKTYPE.LOCK_ONLYONCE
member.summary: If this lock is granted, no other LOCK_ONLYONCE lock can be obtained on the range. Usually this lock type is an alias for some other lock type. Thus, specific implementations can have additional behavior associated with this lock type.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.System.Com.SAFEARRAY
member.summary: Represents a safe array.

remarks.para: The array rgsabound is stored with the left-most dimension in rgsabound[0] and the right-most dimension in rgsabound[cDims - 1]. If an array was specified in a C-like syntax as a [2][5], it would have two elements in the rgsabound vector. Element 0 has an lLbound of 0 and a cElements of 2. Element 1 has an lLbound of 0 and a cElements of 5.

The fFeatures flags describe attributes of an array that can affect how the array is released. The fFeatures field describes what type of data is stored in the SAFEARRAY and how the array is allocated. This allows freeing the array without referencing its containing variant.
Read more on learn.microsoft.com.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.SAFEARRAY.cDims
member.summary: The number of dimensions.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.SAFEARRAY.cbElements
member.summary: The size of an array element.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.SAFEARRAY.cLocks
member.summary: The number of times the array has been locked without a corresponding unlock.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.SAFEARRAY.pvData
member.summary: The data.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.SAFEARRAY.rgsabound
member.summary: One bound for each dimension.

member.summary: Computes the amount of memory that must be allocated to store this struct, including the specified number of elements in the variable length inline array at the end.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.System.Com.STATSTG
member.summary: Contains statistical data about an open storage, stream, or byte-array object.

remarks.para: Learn more about this API from learn.microsoft.com.

summary.para: A pointer to a NULL-terminated Unicode string that contains the name. Space for this string is allocated by the method called and freed by the caller (for more information, see CoTaskMemFree). To not return this member, specify the STATFLAG_NONAME value when you call a method that returns a STATSTG structure, except for calls to IEnumSTATSTG::Next, which provides no way to specify this value.
Indicates the type of storage object. This is one of the values from the STGTY enumeration.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.STATSTG.cbSize
member.summary: Specifies the size, in bytes, of the stream or byte array.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.STATSTG.mtime
member.summary: Indicates the last modification time for this storage, stream, or byte array.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.STATSTG.ctime
member.summary: Indicates the creation time for this storage, stream, or byte array.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.STATSTG.atime
member.summary: Indicates the last access time for this storage, stream, or byte array.

summary.para: Indicates the access mode specified when the object was opened. This member is only valid in calls to Stat methods.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.STATSTG.clsid
member.summary: Indicates the class identifier for the storage object; set to CLSID_NULL for new storage objects. This member is not used for streams or byte arrays.

summary.para: Indicates the current state bits of the storage object; that is, the value most recently set by the IStorage::SetStateBits method. This member is not valid for streams or byte arrays.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.STATSTG.reserved
member.summary: Reserved for future use.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.System.Com.STGM
member.summary: Flags that indicate conditions for creating and deleting the object and access modes for the object.
member.remarks: You can combine these flags, but you can only choose one flag from each group of related flags. Typically one flag from each of the access and sharing groups must be specified for all functions and methods which use these constants. Flags from other groups are optional.

member.summary: The target operating system for the type library is 16-bit Windows. By default, data members are packed.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.SYSKIND.SYS_WIN32
member.summary: The target operating system for the type library is 32-bit Windows. By default, data members are naturally aligned (for example, 2-byte integers are aligned on even-byte boundaries; 4-byte integers are aligned on quad-word boundaries, and so on).

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.SYSKIND.SYS_MAC
member.summary: The target operating system for the type library is Apple Macintosh. By default, all data members are aligned on even-byte boundaries.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.SYSKIND.SYS_WIN64
member.summary: The target operating system for the type library is 64-bit Windows.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.VARFLAGS.VARFLAG_FREQUESTEDIT

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.VARFLAGS.VARFLAG_FDISPLAYBIND
member.summary: The variable is displayed to the user as bindable. VARFLAG_FBINDABLE must also be set.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.VARFLAGS.VARFLAG_FDEFAULTBIND
member.summary: The variable is the single property that best represents the object. Only one variable in type information can have this attribute.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.VARFLAGS.VARFLAG_FHIDDEN
member.summary: The variable should not be displayed to the user in a browser, although it exists and is bindable.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.VARFLAGS.VARFLAG_FRESTRICTED
member.summary: The variable should not be accessible from macro languages. This flag is intended for system-level variables or variables that you do not want type browsers to display.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.VARFLAGS.VARFLAG_FDEFAULTCOLLELEM
member.summary: Permits an optimization in which the compiler looks for a member named "xyz" on the type of abc. If such a member is found and is flagged as an accessor function for an element of the default collection, then a call is generated to that member function. Permitted on members in dispinterfaces and interfaces; not permitted on modules.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.VARFLAGS.VARFLAG_FUIDEFAULT
member.summary: The variable is the default display in the user interface.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.VARFLAGS.VARFLAG_FNONBROWSABLE
member.summary: The variable appears in an object browser, but not in a properties browser.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.VARFLAGS.VARFLAG_FREPLACEABLE
member.summary: Tags the interface as having default behaviors.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.System.Com.VARFLAGS.VARFLAG_FIMMEDIATEBIND
member.summary: The variable is mapped as individual bindable properties.

member.summary: The DECIMAL structure represents a decimal data type that provides a sign and scale for a number.

remarks.para: The **HRESULT** data type is the same as the [SCODE](scode.md) data type. An **HRESULT** value consists of the following fields: - A 1-bit code indicating severity, where zero represents success and 1 represents failure. - A 4-bit reserved value. - An 11-bit code indicating responsibility for the error or warning, also known as a facility code. - A 16-bit code describing the error or warning. Most MAPI interface methods and functions return **HRESULT** values to provide detailed cause formation. **HRESULT** values are also used widely in OLE interface methods. OLE provides several macros for converting between **HRESULT** values and **SCODE** values, another common data type for error handling. > [!NOTE] > In 64-bit MAPI, **HRESULT** is still a 32-bit value. For information about the OLE use of **HRESULT** values, see the *OLE Programmer's Reference*. For more information about the use of these values in MAPI, see [Error Handling](error-handling-in-mapi.md) and any of the following interface methods: [IABLogon::GetLastError](iablogon-getlasterror.md) [IMAPISupport::GetLastError](imapisupport-getlasterror.md) [IMAPIControl::GetLastError](imapicontrol-getlasterror.md) [IMAPITable::GetLastError](imapitable-getlasterror.md) [IMAPIProp::GetLastError](imapiprop-getlasterror.md) [IMAPIViewAdviseSink::OnPrint](imapiviewadvisesink-onprint.md)
Read more on learn.microsoft.com.

member.name: M:Windows.Win32.Foundation.HRESULT.ThrowOnFailure(System.IntPtr)
member.param: A pointer to the IErrorInfo interface that provides more information about the error. You can specify to use the current IErrorInfo interface, or new IntPtr(-1) to ignore the current IErrorInfo interface and construct the exception just from the error code.
member.returns: , if it does not reflect an error.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BIND_FLAG
member.summary: Identifies how to bind a resource to the pipeline. (D3D11_BIND_FLAG)

remarks.para: In general, binding flags can be combined using a bitwise OR (except the constant-buffer flag); however, you should use a single flag to allow the device to optimize the resource usage. This enumeration is used by a:
This doc was truncated.
Read more on learn.microsoft.com.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BIND_FLAG.D3D11_BIND_VERTEX_BUFFER
member.summary: Bind a buffer as a vertex buffer to the input-assembler stage.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BIND_FLAG.D3D11_BIND_INDEX_BUFFER
member.summary: Bind a buffer as an index buffer to the input-assembler stage.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BIND_FLAG.D3D11_BIND_CONSTANT_BUFFER
member.summary: Bind a buffer as a constant buffer to a shader stage; this flag may NOT be combined with any other bind flag.

summary.para:
Bind a buffer or texture to a shader stage; this flag cannot be used with the D3D11_MAP_WRITE_NO_OVERWRITE flag.
Note The Direct3D 11.1 runtime, which is available starting with Windows 8, enables mapping dynamic constant buffers and shader resource views (SRVs) of dynamic buffers with D3D11_MAP_WRITE_NO_OVERWRITE. The Direct3D 11 and earlier runtimes limited mapping to vertex or index buffers. To determine if a Direct3D device supports these features, call ID3D11Device::CheckFeatureSupport with D3D11_FEATURE_D3D11_OPTIONS. CheckFeatureSupport fills members of a D3D11_FEATURE_DATA_D3D11_OPTIONS structure with the device's features. The relevant members here are MapNoOverwriteOnDynamicConstantBuffer and MapNoOverwriteOnDynamicBufferSRV.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BIND_FLAG.D3D11_BIND_STREAM_OUTPUT
member.summary: Bind an output buffer for the stream-output stage.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BIND_FLAG.D3D11_BIND_RENDER_TARGET
member.summary: Bind a texture as a render target for the output-merger stage.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BIND_FLAG.D3D11_BIND_DEPTH_STENCIL
member.summary: Bind a texture as a depth-stencil target for the output-merger stage.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BIND_FLAG.D3D11_BIND_UNORDERED_ACCESS
member.summary: Bind an unordered access resource.

summary.para: Set this flag to indicate that a 2D texture is used to receive output from the decoder API. The common way to create resources for a decoder output is by calling the ID3D11Device::CreateTexture2D method to create an array of 2D textures. However, you cannot use texture arrays that are created with this flag in calls to ID3D11Device::CreateShaderResourceView. Direct3D 11: This value is not supported until Direct3D 11.1.

Set this flag to indicate that a 2D texture is used to receive input from the video encoder API. The common way to create resources for a video encoder is by calling the ID3D11Device::CreateTexture2D method to create an array of 2D textures. However, you cannot use texture arrays that are created with this flag in calls to ID3D11Device::CreateShaderResourceView. Direct3D 11: This value is not supported until Direct3D 11.1.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND
member.summary: Blend factors, which modulate values for the pixel shader and render target.
member.remarks: Blend operations are specified in a blend description.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND.D3D11_BLEND_ZERO
member.summary: The blend factor is (0, 0, 0, 0). No pre-blend operation.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND.D3D11_BLEND_ONE
member.summary: The blend factor is (1, 1, 1, 1). No pre-blend operation.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND.D3D11_BLEND_SRC_COLOR
member.summary: The blend factor is (Rₛ, Gₛ, Bₛ, Aₛ), that is color data (RGB) from a pixel shader. No pre-blend operation.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND.D3D11_BLEND_INV_SRC_COLOR
member.summary: The blend factor is (1 - Rₛ, 1 - Gₛ, 1 - Bₛ, 1 - Aₛ), that is color data (RGB) from a pixel shader. The pre-blend operation inverts the data, generating 1 - RGB.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND.D3D11_BLEND_SRC_ALPHA
member.summary: The blend factor is (Aₛ, Aₛ, Aₛ, Aₛ), that is alpha data (A) from a pixel shader. No pre-blend operation.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND.D3D11_BLEND_INV_SRC_ALPHA
member.summary: The blend factor is ( 1 - Aₛ, 1 - Aₛ, 1 - Aₛ, 1 - Aₛ), that is alpha data (A) from a pixel shader. The pre-blend operation inverts the data, generating 1 - A.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND.D3D11_BLEND_DEST_ALPHA
member.summary: The blend factor is (Ad Ad Ad Ad), that is alpha data from a render target. No pre-blend operation.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND.D3D11_BLEND_INV_DEST_ALPHA
member.summary: The blend factor is (1 - Ad 1 - Ad 1 - Ad 1 - Ad), that is alpha data from a render target. The pre-blend operation inverts the data, generating 1 - A.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND.D3D11_BLEND_DEST_COLOR
member.summary: The blend factor is (Rd, Gd, Bd, Ad), that is color data from a render target. No pre-blend operation.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND.D3D11_BLEND_INV_DEST_COLOR
member.summary: The blend factor is (1 - Rd, 1 - Gd, 1 - Bd, 1 - Ad), that is color data from a render target. The pre-blend operation inverts the data, generating 1 - RGB.

summary.para: The blend factor is (f, f, f, 1); where f = min(Aₛ, 1 - Ad). The pre-blend operation clamps the data to 1 or less.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND.D3D11_BLEND_BLEND_FACTOR
member.summary: The blend factor is the blend factor set with ID3D11DeviceContext::OMSetBlendState. No pre-blend operation.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND.D3D11_BLEND_INV_BLEND_FACTOR
member.summary: The blend factor is the blend factor set with ID3D11DeviceContext::OMSetBlendState. The pre-blend operation inverts the blend factor, generating 1 - blend_factor.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND.D3D11_BLEND_SRC1_COLOR
member.summary: The blend factor is data sources both as color data output by a pixel shader. There is no pre-blend operation. This blend factor supports dual-source color blending.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND.D3D11_BLEND_INV_SRC1_COLOR
member.summary: The blend factor is data sources both as color data output by a pixel shader. The pre-blend operation inverts the data, generating 1 - RGB. This blend factor supports dual-source color blending.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND.D3D11_BLEND_SRC1_ALPHA
member.summary: The blend factor is data sources as alpha data output by a pixel shader. There is no pre-blend operation. This blend factor supports dual-source color blending.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND.D3D11_BLEND_INV_SRC1_ALPHA
member.summary: The blend factor is data sources as alpha data output by a pixel shader. The pre-blend operation inverts the data, generating 1 - A. This blend factor supports dual-source color blending.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND_DESC
member.summary: Describes the blend state that you use in a call to ID3D11Device::CreateBlendState to create a blend-state object.

remarks.para: Here are the default values for blend state.
This doc was truncated.
Read more on learn.microsoft.com.

summary.para: Type: BOOL Specifies whether to use alpha-to-coverage as a multisampling technique when setting a pixel to a render target. For more info about using alpha-to-coverage, see Alpha-To-Coverage.

Type: BOOL Specifies whether to enable independent blending in simultaneous render targets. Set to TRUE to enable independent blending. If set to FALSE, only the RenderTarget[0] members are used; RenderTarget[1..7] are ignored.

Type: D3D11_RENDER_TARGET_BLEND_DESC[8] An array of D3D11_RENDER_TARGET_BLEND_DESC structures that describe the blend states for render targets; these correspond to the eight render targets that can be bound to the output-merger stage at one time.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND_OP
member.summary: RGB or alpha blending operation. (D3D11_BLEND_OP)

remarks.para: The runtime implements RGB blending and alpha blending separately. Therefore, blend state requires separate blend operations for RGB data and alpha data. These blend operations are specified in a blend description. The two sources —source 1 and source 2— are shown in the blending block diagram. Blend state is used by the output-merger stage to determine how to blend together two RGB pixel values and two alpha values. The two RGB pixel values and two alpha values are the RGB pixel value and alpha value that the pixel shader outputs and the RGB pixel value and alpha value already in the output render target. The blend option controls the data source that the blending stage uses to modulate values for the pixel shader, render target, or both. The blend operation controls how the blending stage mathematically combines these modulated values.
Read more on learn.microsoft.com.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND_OP.D3D11_BLEND_OP_ADD
member.summary: Add source 1 and source 2.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND_OP.D3D11_BLEND_OP_SUBTRACT
member.summary: Subtract source 1 from source 2.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND_OP.D3D11_BLEND_OP_REV_SUBTRACT
member.summary: Subtract source 2 from source 1.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND_OP.D3D11_BLEND_OP_MIN
member.summary: Find the minimum of source 1 and source 2.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BLEND_OP.D3D11_BLEND_OP_MAX
member.summary: Find the maximum of source 1 and source 2.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BUFFEREX_SRV
member.summary: Describes the elements in a raw buffer resource to use in a shader-resource view.
member.remarks: This structure is used by D3D11_SHADER_RESOURCE_VIEW_DESC to create a raw view of a buffer.

summary.para: Type: UINT The index of the first element to be accessed by the view.
Type: UINT The number of elements in the resource.

Type: UINT A D3D11_BUFFEREX_SRV_FLAG-typed value that identifies view options for the buffer. Currently, the only option is to identify a raw view of the buffer. For more info about raw viewing of buffers, see Raw Views of Buffers.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BUFFER_DESC
member.summary: Describes a buffer resource. (D3D11_BUFFER_DESC)

remarks.para: This structure is used by ID3D11Device::CreateBuffer to create buffer resources. In addition to this structure, you can also use the CD3D11_BUFFER_DESC derived structure, which is defined in D3D11.h and behaves like an inherited class, to help create a buffer description. If the bind flag is D3D11_BIND_CONSTANT_BUFFER, you must set the ByteWidth value in multiples of 16, and less than or equal to D3D11_REQ_CONSTANT_BUFFER_ELEMENT_COUNT.
Read more on learn.microsoft.com.

summary.para: Type: UINT Size of the buffer in bytes.

Type: D3D11_USAGE Identify how the buffer is expected to be read from and written to. Frequency of update is a key factor. The most common value is typically D3D11_USAGE_DEFAULT; see D3D11_USAGE for all possible values.

Type: UINT The size of each element in the buffer structure (in bytes) when the buffer represents a structured buffer. For more info about structured buffers, see Structured Buffer. The size value in StructureByteStride must match the size of the format that you use for views of the buffer. For example, if you use a shader resource view (SRV) to read a buffer in a pixel shader, the SRV format size must match the size value in StructureByteStride.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BUFFER_RTV
member.summary: Specifies the elements in a buffer resource to use in a render-target view.
member.remarks: A render-target view is a member of a render-target-view description (see D3D11_RENDER_TARGET_VIEW_DESC). Create a render-target view by calling ID3D11Device::CreateRenderTargetView.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BUFFER_SRV
member.summary: Specifies the elements in a buffer resource to use in a shader-resource view. (D3D11_BUFFER_SRV)
member.remarks: The D3D11_BUFFER_SRV structure is a member of the D3D11_SHADER_RESOURCE_VIEW_DESC structure, which represents a shader-resource view description. You can create a shader-resource view by calling the ID3D11Device::CreateShaderResourceView method.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_BUFFER_UAV
member.summary: Describes the elements in a buffer to use in a unordered-access view. (D3D11_BUFFER_UAV)
member.remarks: This structure is used by a D3D11_UNORDERED_ACCESS_VIEW_DESC.

summary.para: Type: UINT The zero-based index of the first element to be accessed.
Type: UINT The number of elements in the resource. For structured buffers, this is the number of structures in the buffer.
Type: UINT View options for the resource (see D3D11_BUFFER_UAV_FLAG).

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_CLASS_INSTANCE_DESC
member.summary: Describes an HLSL class instance.

remarks.para: The D3D11_CLASS_INSTANCE_DESC structure is returned by the ID3D11ClassInstance::GetDesc method. The members of this structure except InstanceIndex are valid (non default values) if they describe a class instance acquired using ID3D11ClassLinkage::CreateClassInstance. The InstanceIndex member is only valid when the class instance is aquired using ID3D11ClassLinkage::GetClassInstance.
Read more on learn.microsoft.com.

summary.para: Type: UINT The instance ID of an HLSL class; the default value is 0.
Type: UINT The instance index of an HLSL class; the default value is 0.
Type: UINT The type ID of an HLSL class; the default value is 0.
Type: UINT Describes the constant buffer associated with an HLSL class; the default value is 0.
Type: UINT The base constant buffer offset associated with an HLSL class; the default value is 0.
Type: UINT The base texture associated with an HLSL class; the default value is 127.
Type: UINT The base sampler associated with an HLSL class; the default value is 15.
Type: BOOL True if the class was created; the default value is false.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_COMPARISON_FUNC
member.summary: Comparison options. (D3D11_COMPARISON_FUNC)
member.remarks: A comparison option determines whether how the runtime compares source (new) data against destination (existing) data before storing the new data. The comparison option is declared in a description before an object is created. The API allows you to set a comparison option for a depth-stencil buffer (see D3D11_DEPTH_STENCIL_DESC), depth-stencil operations (see D3D11_DEPTH_STENCILOP_DESC), or sampler state (see D3D11_SAMPLER_DESC).

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_COMPARISON_FUNC.D3D11_COMPARISON_NEVER
member.summary: Never pass the comparison.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_COMPARISON_FUNC.D3D11_COMPARISON_LESS
member.summary: If the source data is less than the destination data, the comparison passes.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_COMPARISON_FUNC.D3D11_COMPARISON_EQUAL
member.summary: If the source data is equal to the destination data, the comparison passes.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_COMPARISON_FUNC.D3D11_COMPARISON_LESS_EQUAL
member.summary: If the source data is less than or equal to the destination data, the comparison passes.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_COMPARISON_FUNC.D3D11_COMPARISON_GREATER
member.summary: If the source data is greater than the destination data, the comparison passes.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_COMPARISON_FUNC.D3D11_COMPARISON_NOT_EQUAL
member.summary: If the source data is not equal to the destination data, the comparison passes.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_COMPARISON_FUNC.D3D11_COMPARISON_GREATER_EQUAL
member.summary: If the source data is greater than or equal to the destination data, the comparison passes.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_COMPARISON_FUNC.D3D11_COMPARISON_ALWAYS
member.summary: Always pass the comparison.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_COUNTER

Read more on learn.microsoft.com.

member.summary: Define a performance counter that is dependent on the hardware device.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_COUNTER_DESC
member.summary: Describes a counter. (D3D11_COUNTER_DESC)
member.remarks: This structure is used by ID3D11Counter::GetDesc, ID3D11Device::CheckCounter and ID3D11Device::CreateCounter.

summary.para: Type: D3D11_COUNTER Type of counter (see D3D11_COUNTER).

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_COUNTER_INFO
member.summary: Information about the video card's performance counter capabilities. (D3D11_COUNTER_INFO)
member.remarks: This structure is returned by ID3D11Device::CheckCounterInfo.

summary.para: Type: D3D11_COUNTER Largest device-dependent counter ID that the device supports. If none are supported, this value will be 0. Otherwise it will be greater than or equal to D3D11_COUNTER_DEVICE_DEPENDENT_0. See D3D11_COUNTER.
Type: UINT Number of counters that can be simultaneously supported.

Type: UINT8 Number of detectable parallel units that the counter is able to discern. Values are 1 ~ 4. Use NumDetectableParallelUnits to interpret the values of the VERTEX_PROCESSING, GEOMETRY_PROCESSING, PIXEL_PROCESSING, and OTHER_GPU_PROCESSING counters.

member.summary: Data type of a performance counter. (D3D11_COUNTER_TYPE)

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_CPU_ACCESS_FLAG
member.summary: Specifies the types of CPU access allowed for a resource. (D3D11_CPU_ACCESS_FLAG)

remarks.para: This enumeration is used in D3D11_BUFFER_DESC, D3D11_TEXTURE1D_DESC, D3D11_TEXTURE2D_DESC, D3D11_TEXTURE3D_DESC. Applications may combine one or more of these flags with a bitwise OR. When possible, create resources with no CPU access flags, as this enables better resource optimization. The D3D11_RESOURCE_MISC_FLAG cannot be used when creating resources with D3D11_CPU_ACCESS flags.
Read more on learn.microsoft.com.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_CPU_ACCESS_FLAG.D3D11_CPU_ACCESS_WRITE
member.summary: The resource is to be mappable so that the CPU can change its contents. Resources created with this flag cannot be set as outputs of the pipeline and must be created with either dynamic or staging usage (see D3D11_USAGE).

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_CPU_ACCESS_FLAG.D3D11_CPU_ACCESS_READ
member.summary: The resource is to be mappable so that the CPU can read its contents. Resources created with this flag cannot be set as either inputs or outputs to the pipeline and must be created with staging usage (see D3D11_USAGE).

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_CREATE_DEVICE_FLAG
member.summary: Describes parameters that are used to create a device.

remarks.para: Device creation flags are used by D3D11CreateDevice and D3D11CreateDeviceAndSwapChain. An application might dynamically create (and destroy) threads to improve performance especially on a machine with multiple CPU cores. There may be cases, however, when an application needs to prevent extra threads from being created. This can happen when you want to simplify debugging, profile code or develop a tool for instance. For these cases, use D3D11_CREATE_DEVICE_PREVENT_INTERNAL_THREADING_OPTIMIZATIONS to request that the runtime and video driver not create any additional threads that might interfere with the application.
Read more on learn.microsoft.com.

summary.para: Use this flag if your application will only call methods of Direct3D 11 interfaces from a single thread. By default, the ID3D11Device object is thread-safe. By using this flag, you can increase performance. However, if you use this flag and your application calls methods of Direct3D 11 interfaces from multiple threads, undefined behavior might result.

Creates a device that supports the debug layer. To use this flag, you must have D3D11*SDKLayers.dll installed; otherwise, device creation fails. To get D3D11_1SDKLayers.dll, install the SDK for Windows 8.

para.div: Note This flag is not supported in Direct3D 11.

summary.para: Prevents multiple threads from being created. When this flag is used with a Windows Advanced Rasterization Platform (WARP) device, no additional threads will be created by WARP and all rasterization will occur on the calling thread. This flag is not recommended for general use. See remarks.

Creates a device that supports BGRA formats (DXGI_FORMAT_B8G8R8A8_UNORM and DXGI_FORMAT_B8G8R8A8_UNORM_SRGB). All 10level9 and higher hardware with WDDM 1.1+ drivers support BGRA formats.
Note Required for Direct2D interoperability with Direct3D resources.

Causes the device and driver to keep information that you can use for shader debugging. The exact impact from this flag will vary from driver to driver. To use this flag, you must have D3D11_1SDKLayers.dll installed; otherwise, device creation fails. The created device supports the debug layer. To get D3D11_1SDKLayers.dll, install the SDK for Windows 8. If you use this flag and the current driver does not support shader debugging, device creation fails. Shader debugging requires a driver that is implemented to the WDDM for Windows 8 (WDDM 1.2). Direct3D 11: This value is not supported until Direct3D 11.1.

Causes the Direct3D runtime to ignore registry settings that turn on the debug layer. You can turn on the debug layer by using the DirectX Control Panel that was included as part of the DirectX SDK. We shipped the last version of the DirectX SDK in June 2010; you can download it from the Microsoft Download Center. You can set this flag in your app, typically in release builds only, to prevent end users from using the DirectX Control Panel to monitor how the app uses Direct3D.
Note You can also set this flag in your app to prevent Direct3D debugging tools, such as Visual Studio Ultimate 2012, from hooking your app.
Windows 8.1: This flag doesn't prevent Visual Studio 2013 and later running on Windows 8.1 and later from hooking your app; instead use ID3D11DeviceContext2::IsAnnotationEnabled. This flag still prevents Visual Studio 2013 and later running on Windows 8 and earlier from hooking your app. Direct3D 11: This value is not supported until Direct3D 11.1.

Use this flag if the device will produce GPU workloads that take more than two seconds to complete, and you want the operating system to allow them to successfully finish. If this flag is not set, the operating system performs timeout detection and recovery when it detects a GPU packet that took more than two seconds to execute. If this flag is set, the operating system allows such a long running packet to execute without resetting the GPU. We recommend not to set this flag if your device needs to be highly responsive so that the operating system can detect and recover from GPU timeouts. We recommend to set this flag if your device needs to perform time consuming background tasks such as compute, image recognition, and video encoding to allow such tasks to successfully finish. Direct3D 11: This value is not supported until Direct3D 11.1.

Forces the creation of the Direct3D device to fail if the display driver is not implemented to the WDDM for Windows 8 (WDDM 1.2). When the display driver is not implemented to WDDM 1.2, only a Direct3D device that is created with feature level 9.1, 9.2, or 9.3 supports video; therefore, if this flag is set, the runtime creates the Direct3D device only for feature level 9.1, 9.2, or 9.3. We recommend not to specify this flag for applications that want to favor Direct3D capability over video. If feature level 10 and higher is available, the runtime will use that feature level regardless of video support. If this flag is set, device creation on the Basic Render Device (BRD) will succeed regardless of the BRD's missing support for video decode. This is because the Media Foundation video stack operates in software mode on BRD. In this situation, if you force the video stack to create the Direct3D device twice (create the device once with this flag, next discover BRD, then again create the device without the flag), you actually degrade performance. If you attempt to create a Direct3D device with driver type D3D_DRIVER_TYPE_NULL, D3D_DRIVER_TYPE_REFERENCE, or D3D_DRIVER_TYPE_SOFTWARE, device creation fails at any feature level because none of the associated drivers provide video capability. If you attempt to create a Direct3D device with driver type D3D_DRIVER_TYPE_WARP, device creation succeeds to allow software fallback for video. Direct3D 11: This value is not supported until Direct3D 11.1.

member.summary: Indicates triangles facing a particular direction are not drawn. (D3D11_CULL_MODE)

member.summary: Always draw all triangles.

member.summary: Do not draw triangles that are front-facing.

member.name: F:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_CULL_MODE.D3D11_CULL_BACK

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_DEPTH_STENCILOP_DESC
member.summary: Stencil operations that can be performed based on the results of stencil test.

remarks.para: All stencil operations are specified as a D3D11_STENCIL_OP. The stencil operation can be set differently based on the outcome of the stencil test (which is referred to as StencilFunc in the stencil test portion of depth-stencil testing. This structure is a member of a depth-stencil description.
Read more on learn.microsoft.com.

summary.para: Type: D3D11_STENCIL_OP The stencil operation to perform when stencil testing fails.
Type: D3D11_STENCIL_OP The stencil operation to perform when stencil testing passes and depth testing fails.
Type: D3D11_STENCIL_OP The stencil operation to perform when stencil testing and depth testing both pass.
Type: D3D11_COMPARISON_FUNC A function that compares stencil data against existing stencil data. The function options are listed in D3D11_COMPARISON_FUNC.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_DEPTH_STENCIL_DESC
member.summary: Describes depth-stencil state. (D3D11_DEPTH_STENCIL_DESC)

remarks.para: Pass a pointer to D3D11_DEPTH_STENCIL_DESC to the ID3D11Device::CreateDepthStencilState method to create the depth-stencil state object. Depth-stencil state controls how depth-stencil testing is performed by the output-merger stage. The following table shows the default values of depth-stencil states.
This doc was truncated.
Read more on learn.microsoft.com.

summary.para: Type: D3D11_DEPTH_WRITE_MASK Identify a portion of the depth-stencil buffer that can be modified by depth data (see D3D11_DEPTH_WRITE_MASK).
Type: D3D11_COMPARISON_FUNC A function that compares depth data against existing depth data. The function options are listed in D3D11_COMPARISON_FUNC.
Type: UINT8 Identify a portion of the depth-stencil buffer for reading stencil data.
Type: UINT8 Identify a portion of the depth-stencil buffer for writing stencil data.
Type: D3D11_DEPTH_STENCILOP_DESC Identify how to use the results of the depth test and the stencil test for pixels whose surface normal is facing towards the camera (see D3D11_DEPTH_STENCILOP_DESC).

Type: D3D11_DEPTH_STENCILOP_DESC Identify how to use the results of the depth test and the stencil test for pixels whose surface normal is facing away from the camera (see D3D11_DEPTH_STENCILOP_DESC).

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_DEPTH_STENCIL_VIEW_DESC
member.summary: Specifies the subresources of a texture that are accessible from a depth-stencil view.

remarks.para: These are valid formats for a depth-stencil view:
This doc was truncated.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_FEATURE
member.summary: Direct3D 11 feature options.
member.remarks: This enumeration is used when querying a driver about support for these features by calling ID3D11Device::CheckFeatureSupport. Each value in this enumeration has a corresponding data structure that is required to be passed to the pFeatureSupportData parameter of ID3D11Device::CheckFeatureSupport.

Supports Direct3D 11.3 conservative rasterization feature options. Refer to D3D11_FEATURE_DATA_D3D11_OPTIONS2. Direct3D 11: This value is not supported until Direct3D 11.3.

member.name: T:Windows.Win32.Graphics.Direct3D11.D3D11_FILTER
member.summary: Filtering options during texture sampling. (D3D11_FILTER)

remarks.para:
Note If you use different filter types for min versus mag filter, undefined behavior occurs in certain cases where the choice between whether magnification or minification happens is ambiguous. To prevent this undefined behavior, use filter modes that use similar filter operations for both min and mag (or use anisotropic filtering, which avoids the issue as well).
During texture sampling, one or more texels are read and combined (this is calling filtering) to produce a single value. Point sampling reads a single texel while linear sampling reads two texels (endpoints) and linearly interpolates a third value between the endpoints. HLSL texture-sampling functions also support comparison filtering during texture sampling. Comparison filtering compares each sampled texel against a comparison value. The boolean result is blended the same way that normal texture filtering is blended. You can use HLSL intrinsic texture-sampling functions that implement texture filtering only or companion functions that use texture filtering with comparison filtering.
This doc was truncated.
Read more on learn.microsoft.com.
